text: Yeah, this is a problem.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You're generally going to want to try to make sure that the JDK that you're using on the command line is going to be the same JDK that your builds from Android Studio run.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And there's a couple of reasons for that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: One, you want consistency.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You don't want a build that doesn't work from the command line and works in Android Studio.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And that could be a frustration for sure.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But also, if you even have a slightly different version, even if they're both JDK 17, let's say you have two installs somewhere on your disk, the Android Studio and the command line Gradle builds that you do might end up spawning more than one Gradle daemon because they have different JDKs that they're using or different JDK options passed into those JDKs.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And there's a way in Android Studio, if you do the find actions command in Android Studio, you can show Gradle daemons and see if you're running more than one, because that can be a big use of memory, both on your local machine.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You also want to make sure on a CI machine that you're not spawning more than one Gradle daemon at a time, because that can be using memory.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And Gradle, sort of left to its own devices, will always sort of check before it uses a Gradle daemon.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then you can tell Android Studio to try to pick it up from your Java home as well.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And as of the recent versions of Kotlin and the recent versions of Android Gradle plugin...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: they're all toolchain-aware, which means they look at what toolchain you've set for that subproject, and they'll pick it up, and they'll go and use that version of the SDK.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And Gradle will actually download and install that version if it doesn't already find one laying around on your system.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So you can actually say 17, and if your Gradle build's running on 11...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: and you say toolchain 17, it's going to go look around for 17.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If it doesn't find one, it's going to download and cache a version of JDK 17 for you.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then it's going to use that to run the Java compiler and the Kotlin compiler and any other tasks that are toolchain aware within your Gradle build.
speaker: SPEAKER_01
